intro: Hey, welcome back to The Weekly Rundown — this week did not mess around, with Bellingham bailing England out against Norway, Sinner locking up another Wimbledon title in a five-set war, and Tom Kim finally snapping a 1,001-day winless streak with a flawless round in Scotland. Oh, and Giannis is a Miami Heat now, because of course he is.

stories.headline: Bellingham Does It Again: England Survive Norway Scare to Reach the Semis
stories.body: Jude Bellingham said 'not today' to Norway's World Cup dreams, scoring twice in extra time to send England through 2–1 in a nervy quarter-final. Now England face Argentina on Wednesday in Atlanta, which means we're one step away from a final that would break the entire internet. Oh, and Bellingham apparently had some words for manager Thomas Tuchel after the game — so the drama is very much on and off the pitch.

stories.headline: Messi Assists, Alvarez Dazzles: Argentina Are Coming for England
stories.body: Lionel Messi didn't get on the scoresheet against Switzerland, but he didn't need to — he set up Julian Alvarez for a stunning goal in a comfortable 3–1 win that has Argentina locked in for a semi-final date with England. Switzerland's afternoon got even worse when Breel Embolo got sent off for diving, which is honestly a rough way to go out. England vs. Argentina in Atlanta on Wednesday — yeah, no big deal at all.

stories.headline: Andrey Santos Ditches Chelsea for Old Trafford in £50M Switch
stories.body: While the World Cup chaos dominates the headlines, Manchester United quietly made some noise by officially confirming the signing of Brazilian midfielder Andrey Santos from Chelsea for £50 million. It's a statement move from United as they try to rebuild in the post-World Cup transfer window, and Chelsea fans probably aren't thrilled about strengthening a rival. In other cross-town business, Man City also snapped up England's Niamh Charles from Chelsea for £500,000 — Blues are having a rough week in the transfer market.

stories.headline: Sinner Does It Again: Italian Ice Defends Wimbledon Crown in Five-Set Thriller
stories.body: Jannik Sinner picked up his fifth Grand Slam title and second straight Wimbledon crown, outlasting Alexander Zverev 6–7, 7–6, 6–3, 6–4 in a final that had no business being as dramatic as it was. Sinner dropped the first set in a tiebreak and then just... took over, because that's apparently what he does now. Zverev made it to his first-ever Wimbledon final and gave it everything he had — unfortunately for him, everything he had wasn't quite enough.

stories.headline: Linda Noskova Crashes the Party: 21-Year-Old Czech Stuns Wimbledon to Claim First Slam
stories.body: Linda Noskova is a Grand Slam champion, and if you're not familiar with her yet, get familiar fast. The 21-year-old Czech dispatched fellow countrywoman Karolina Muchova 6–2, 5–7, 6–3 to lift the Venus Rosewater Dish and announce herself to the world in the biggest way possible. Muchova had already knocked out Naomi Osaka on her way to the final, so this was no easy path — Noskova just made it look that way.

stories.headline: Djokovic's Record Hunt Hits a Wall — Again — as Sinner Shuts the Door in Straights
stories.body: Novak Djokovic's quest for a record-breaking 25th Grand Slam title came up short once more, with Sinner sending him packing 6–4, 6–4, 6–4 in the semifinals. It's worth noting Djokovic had already survived a grueling 5-hour-15-minute quarterfinal against Felix Auger-Aliassime just to get there, which makes the loss a little easier to stomach — a little. Meanwhile, Naomi Osaka reached her first-ever Wimbledon quarterfinal after upsetting top-ranked Aryna Sabalenka, and Coco Gauff made her first Wimbledon semifinal, so the women's draw had no shortage of storylines either.

stories.headline: Tom Kim Ends a 1,001-Day Drought With a Bogey-Free Masterclass in Scotland
stories.body: Tom Kim didn't just win the Genesis Scottish Open — he did it with a clean, bogey-free 64 that left the Renaissance Club field two shots back in the rearview mirror. It's his 4th PGA Tour win and his first since fall 2023, which means he made about 1,001 days of waiting look very, very worth it. For the record, he's now the 5th player outside the U.S. to rack up four wins before age 25, so the kid is doing just fine.

stories.headline: Haeran Ryu Shot a 60 in a Major and Then Won Another One — Casual
stories.body: Haeran Ryu posted a third-round 60 at the Amundi Evian Championship — the lowest single round in major championship history, men's or women's — and then went ahead and won the whole thing in a playoff over Brooke Henderson. Oh, and she also won the KPMG Women's PGA just 14 days earlier, making this her second consecutive major. At some point you just have to put the clipboard down and applaud.

stories.headline: Chris Gotterup Erases a 5-Shot Deficit on Sunday Because Why Not
stories.body: Chris Gotterup trailed by five shots heading into Sunday at the John Deere Classic, then went out and shot a no-bogey 62 like it was a Tuesday practice round. He got a little help when Ben Kohles dunked one in the water on 18 — a par there would've forced a playoff — but Gotterup will absolutely take it. That's his third win of the 2026 season, and at this rate someone should probably just start engraving his name on things.

stories.headline: Giannis Packs His Bags for South Beach in the Trade Nobody Saw Coming (But Also Everyone Saw Coming)
stories.body: After a year of will-he-won't-he drama, Giannis Antetokounmpo is officially a Miami Heat, heading south in exchange for Tyler Herro, Jaime Jaquez Jr., and a stack of first-round picks. The Eastern Conference just got a whole lot more interesting, and Pat Riley is out here collecting MVP trophies like infinity stones. Oh, and Jaylen Brown quietly slipped out the back door to Philadelphia in the same news cycle, because apparently one earth-shattering trade just wasn't enough for one week.

stories.headline: LeBron James Does LeBron James Things, Keeps Entire League on Edge
stories.body: LeBron has officially told the Lakers he's exploring free agency for next season, because why would the king ever make anything simple. The Warriors are reportedly circling, which means we might actually have to entertain the idea of 41-year-old LeBron running it back in Golden State — and honestly, stranger things have happened this week alone. He says he'd still return to LA for one more year if it comes to that, so Lakers fans can exhale, just not too much.

stories.headline: Kawhi to Toronto Trade Hits a Red Light as NBA Launches Cap Investigation into Clippers
stories.body: The Kawhi Leonard reunion tour with the Raptors is officially on pause, with the NBA holding up the trade while it investigates the Clippers for alleged salary-cap circumvention — which, if true, is a very Clippers way to blow up your own offseason. Meanwhile Kawhi is just out there in basketball purgatory, waiting to find out which city he's playing in, which is somehow very on-brand for him. The Raptors did at least get a feel-good moment this week when Kyle Lowry signed a one-day contract to retire as a franchise legend, so Toronto has that going for them.

stories.headline: Bobrovsky Ditches Florida Sun for Toronto Pressure Cooker
stories.body: Two-time Stanley Cup champion Sergei Bobrovsky is heading to Toronto on a 3-year, $7M AAV deal, and new Leafs GM John Chayka is clearly not messing around in his first big offseason. Leafs fans have been burned by goaltending before, so bringing in a guy with two rings is either a genius move or a very expensive way to find out. Either way, the pressure in Toronto is a whole different beast than Sunrise, Florida — good luck, Sergei.

stories.headline: Anaheim Locks Up Leo Carlsson for a Cool $90 Million — Philly Never Stood a Chance
stories.body: Leo Carlsson is staying a Duck after signing a massive five-year, $90 million deal, shutting down Philadelphia's offer-sheet attempt before it could get ugly. At $18M AAV, Anaheim is betting big that Carlsson is a true franchise cornerstone, and honestly, the Flyers making a run at him tells you everything about how the league values this kid. The rebuild in Anaheim is starting to look very, very real.

stories.headline: Nashville Swipes Mavrik Bourque From Dallas Before Free Agency Even Got Started
stories.body: The Predators jumped the line and snagged young center Mavrik Bourque from the Dallas Stars in a trade, refusing to wait around and risk losing him to the open market. Nashville has been quietly building toward relevance again, and grabbing a promising center with upside is exactly the kind of move a team makes when it thinks its window is cracking open. Dallas fans, meanwhile, are probably wondering what the plan is over there.

stories.headline: Ohtani's Arm Says 'Nope' Right Before All-Star Week
stories.body: Shohei Ohtani left Friday's game with a right biceps issue and is now unlikely to pitch in the All-Star Game — which, honestly, is the most Shohei Ohtani thing imaginable, breaking hearts on a national stage. Kyle Schwarber gets the NL leadoff spot in Philly as a result, so at least the home crowd will have someone to cheer for. Fingers crossed the big guy is just being cautious and this isn't anything serious heading into the second half.

stories.headline: Tyler Tolbert Does Something Nobody Has Done in MLB History — Seriously
stories.body: In a wild 16-12 Royals comeback win over the Mets, Kansas City's Tyler Tolbert went 5-for-6 and tied an MLB record with hits in 12 consecutive plate appearances — the kind of stat that makes you do a double take and google it immediately. The game itself was basically a backyard home run derby that got out of hand, but Tolbert's streak is the story everyone's going to be talking about at the All-Star cookout. Genuinely historic stuff buried inside what looked like a Little League box score.

stories.headline: Athletics Fire Pitching Coach After Nine-Game Skid, Stumble Into Break
stories.body: The Oakland Athletics walked into the All-Star break on a brutal nine-game losing streak and decided the best send-off was handing pitching coach Scott Emerson his walking papers on Monday morning. It's the kind of move that says 'something had to give' even if everyone knows a coaching change mid-collapse is more symbolic than surgical. The A's will have a few days to regroup, but they'll need a lot more than a new pitching coach to turn this second half around.

stories.headline: Leclerc Ends Two-Year Drought With a Statement Win at Silverstone
stories.body: Charles Leclerc finally silenced the doubters in the best way possible, taking victory at the British Grand Prix for his first win since 2024. It helped that championship leader Kimi Antonelli — who had pole and was leading late — suffered a mechanical failure and limped home 15th, turning what could have been a forgettable weekend into a full-blown Ferrari party. Lewis Hamilton added a third-place finish to complete the one-two for the Scuderia, and you can bet Fred Vasseur was doing everything in his power to look calm about it.

stories.headline: Antonelli Takes the Sprint, Loses the Plot in the Main Event
stories.body: Kimi Antonelli had the kind of weekend that gives championship leaders nightmares — he won the sprint race, grabbed pole, and then watched his British GP unravel with a mechanical issue that sent him to 15th. The good news for the young Mercedes star is he still leads the standings by 43 points, so the damage was manageable. The bad news is Russell finished second, which means his own teammate is now breathing a little harder down his neck.

stories.headline: Horner's Back in the Paddock, Verstappen's Calling the Wing 'Super-Dangerous'
stories.body: It was a chaotic weekend in the Red Bull garage even before Christian Horner showed up at Silverstone for the first time since being sacked as team boss — the FIA president has apparently declared that Horner 'will' return to the sport, which should make for some very awkward canapé conversations. Meanwhile, Max Verstappen crashed out while fighting for a podium and wasted no time calling Red Bull's rear wing 'super-dangerous,' which is a very loud way of saying the car still isn't where it needs to be. Things are not exactly humming over in Milton Keynes right now.

stories.headline: Someone Just Paid $9.6 Billion for the Seattle Seahawks — Yes, Billion with a B
stories.body: Tech billionaire Vinod Khosla and his ownership group officially bought the reigning Super Bowl champion Seahawks for a record-shattering $9.6 billion, making every previous NFL sale look like a garage sale. The wild subplot here is that Khosla is already a limited partner with the 49ers — Seattle's NFC West rival — which is the kind of thing that makes NFL owners' meetings at least 30% more awkward. The Seahawks will defend their title under brand new ownership, which is either a great omen or a logistical headache, depending on who you ask.

stories.headline: Lions Cut Terrion Arnold After Kidnapping Charges, and Somehow Four Teams Are Already Calling
stories.body: Detroit wasted zero time releasing cornerback Terrion Arnold after he was arrested in Florida and charged with orchestrating the kidnapping and pistol-whipping of three people — charges that carry potential life imprisonment and a $1 million bond. The Lions making the cut is about as unsurprising as it gets, but what IS surprising is that his agent says four teams are interested and the Jets have already worked him out, which tells you everything you need to know about how desperate some franchises are for cornerback help. File this one firmly under 'situations to monitor from a safe distance.'

stories.headline: Mahomes Gets Half a Billion Dollars and Jeffery Simmons Quietly Becomes the Highest-Paid Player in NFL History
stories.body: Patrick Mahomes signed a contract extension worth over $500 million keeping him in Kansas City through 2033, which sounds insane until you remember the Seahawks just sold for $9.6 billion and everything in the NFL is Monopoly money at this point. Meanwhile, Titans defensive tackle Jeffery Simmons quietly walked away with a 3-year, $105.8 million extension that makes him the highest-paid player in NFL history — a sentence that will surprise roughly 90% of casual fans. It's been a big week for people cashing checks.

stories.headline: Oklahoma Ends 32-Year Wait, Beats North Carolina to Claim Baseball Glory
stories.body: The Sooners did it — Oklahoma baseball is back on top for the first time since 1994, routing North Carolina in the final to seal the program's second-ever national championship. It also locked up the SEC's seventh straight baseball title, which at this point is getting a little ridiculous for everyone else in the country.

stories.headline: Texas Lands the No. 1 Player in the Country After Spears Jr. Pulls Off Surprise Reclassification
stories.body: Marcus Spears Jr. was supposed to be a 2027 guy, but he said 'why wait' and reclassified to join Texas immediately — because apparently the Longhorns needed to get even more loaded. The No. 1 player in the 2027 class just skipping a year to burn the country down early is very on-brand for the current era of college football chaos.

stories.headline: Judge Blocks NCAA Age Rule, 15 Players Get an Extra Year Whether the NCAA Likes It or Not
stories.body: A Hamilton County judge threw a wrench into the NCAA's shiny new age-based eligibility model by granting a preliminary injunction that lets 15 athletes play an extra year of college ball. The NCAA just ratified rules to crack down on mid-20s players flooding rosters, and a court immediately said 'hold on' — which is essentially how every NCAA policy story ends these days.
